title: Palmiers
subtitle: Caramelised puff pastry hearts
excerpt: Puff pastry rolled in caster sugar and folded into a heart shape, then baked until the sugar caramelises to a hard, glassy finish. Quick to make with shop-bought all-butter puff and spectacular in proportion to the effort.
sourceNotes: Palmiers are a classic of French pâtisserie, made from leftover puff pastry trimmings. The name refers to their resemblance to a palm leaf. Escoffier documents them in A Guide to Modern Cookery (1907), which entered the public domain. The technique is entirely about folding puff pastry in sugar so the layers hold their shape as the sugar caramelises and the pastry puffs.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape the roll and refrigerate for up to 24 hours before slicing and baking. Baked palmiers are best eaten the same day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more. An oven thermometer takes the guessing out. The caramelisation depends on precise heat.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rolling both long edges of a sheet of sugared puff pastry inward toward the centre simultaneously, until they meet in the middle and the sheet resembles a scroll. Pressing the two rolls together along the centre line locks the shape. When sliced crosswise and viewed from the end, each slice looks like a heart or a palm leaf.

content.text: Palmiers are a one-ingredient biscuit if you use ready-made puff pastry. The sugar caramelises as the pastry bakes, bonding the layers and forming the glassy, brittle finish. All-butter puff pastry makes a noticeably better palmier than a margarine-based one.
Scatter half the sugar onto a clean work surface. Unroll the pastry sheet onto the sugar and press gently. Scatter the remaining sugar over the surface of the pastry. Use the
method: roll both long edges of the pastry inward simultaneously until they meet in the middle. Press the two rolls together to lock the shape. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for 15 minutes.

Preheat the oven to 200°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Using a sharp knife, slice the cold roll into 1 cm rounds and lay them flat on the trays with 5 cm between each; they spread significantly. Bake for 15 to 18 minutes, turning them over halfway through with a palette knife when the base is deep amber. They are done when both sides are a rich, even caramel brown. The sugar must have fully caramelised; a pale palmier is underbaked and sticky rather than crisp. Leave on the tray for 2 minutes, then transfer to a rack before the caramel bonds to the paper.
